title: Introduction to citefrag
text: citefrag package measures within-group versus -group citation preference longitudinal citation networks. vignette walks full analytical pipeline using bundled 23-journal criminology panel example. workflow applies citation panel prior group assignments.

title: Introduction to citefrag
text: panel contains 66 annual citation matrices 23 journals, grouped 4 traditions.
code: library(citefrag) data(criminology_panel)  criminology_panel #> <citation_panel> #>   Periods: 66  (1960 to 2025) #>   Nodes:   23 #>   Groups:  4  (Quant/Empirical, Applied/Practice, Intl/Comparative, Critical/Theoretical) #>   Sizes:   time-varying

title: Introduction to citefrag
text: central measurement package pair-averaged within-citation ratio. year, compares mean number citations journals within tradition mean number journals different traditions.  normalise argument controls size differences journals handled. four options "raw", "row", "target", "article_pair"; answers slightly different question. Row-normalisation gives journal equal weight regardless publication volume.
code: fr <- fragmentation_ratio(criminology_panel, normalise = "row") print(fr) #> <fragmentation_ratio> #>   Periods: 57  (1969 to 2025) #>   Normalisation: row #>   Smoothing:     3-period centred moving average #>   Peak:   R = 3.02  in 1994 #>   Final:  R = 2.01  in 2025 plot(fr)

title: Introduction to citefrag
text: permutation test evaluates whether observed ratio larger expected random assignment journals traditions, holding matrix group sizes fixed.  test exact number distinct permutations feasible enumerate, Monte Carlo otherwise.
code: pt <- fragmentation_test(criminology_panel, n_perm = 1000L, seed = 42L) print(pt) #> <fragmentation_test> #>   Normalisation: raw #>   Periods tested: 57 #>   2025: observed R = 1.84, null mean = 1.01, z = 4.02, p = 0.0060 plot(pt)

title: Introduction to citefrag
text: Two kinds robustness check built . first reruns analysis alternative group specifications, testing whether trajectory depends group boundaries drawn. second restricts panel fixed subset nodes, testing whether trajectory driven entry new nodes.
code: data(criminology_alternatives) rb <- fragmentation_robustness(   criminology_panel,   alternatives = criminology_alternatives ) print(rb) #> <fragmentation_robustness> #>   Normalisation: raw #>   Specifications: 4 (including baseline) #>  #>   Correlations with baseline: #>     merge_cd_to_quant          r = 0.652 (raw), 0.754 (smoothed) #>     merge_ps_to_applied        r = 0.967 (raw), 0.963 (smoothed) #>     merge_cjc_to_applied       r = 0.992 (raw), 0.990 (smoothed) plot(rb)

title: Introduction to citefrag
text: piecewise linear regression grid-searched breakpoint bootstrap confidence interval identifies (anywhere) trajectory changes character.
code: cp <- fragmentation_changepoint(fr, seed = 42L, n_boot = 500L) print(cp) #> <fragmentation_changepoint> #>   Breakpoint:   2003  (95% bootstrap CI: 1974 to 2004) #>   Slope before: +0.0386 per period  (p = < 0.0001) #>   Slope after:  -0.0088 per period  (p = 0.2196) #>   R-squared:    0.671 plot(cp)

title: Introduction to citefrag
text: fragmentation ratio symmetric construction: reveal group cites . asymmetry() function addresses , measuring unevenly citations flow two specified groups time.
code: asy <- asymmetry(   criminology_panel,   from_group = "Critical/Theoretical",   to_group   = "Quant/Empirical" ) print(asy) #> <fragmentation_asymmetry> #>   From group: Critical/Theoretical #>   To group:   Quant/Empirical #>   Periods:    66 #>   2025: ratio = 5.84 (share 0.233 vs 0.040) plot(asy) #> Warning: Removed 41 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_line()`). #> Warning: Removed 41 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range #> (`geom_point()`).

what: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: citefrag measures within-group versus -group citation preference longitudinal journal panels. implements pair-averaged fragmentation ratio, exact Monte Carlo permutation tests, directional asymmetry analysis, suite robustness checks, piecewise regression bootstrap confidence intervals locating structural breaks ratio trajectories. package developed accompany Vakhitova (2026) fragmentation criminology, machinery general longitudinal node--node exchange network priori group labels. works journals grouped research tradition, departments grouped discipline, countries grouped region, comparable setting question whether nodes exchange intensely kind across group boundaries.

title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: can install development version GitHub: package CRAN, installation :
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("zarina-vakhitova/citefrag") install.packages("citefrag")

title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: package ships 23-journal criminology panel spanning 1960 2025, gives real working example box. complete walk-functions applied bundled data, see introductory vignette (vignette("citefrag-intro")).
code: library(citefrag) data(criminology_panel)  # Stage 1: compute the within-cluster to between-cluster ratio fr <- fragmentation_ratio(criminology_panel, normalise = "row") print(fr) plot(fr)  # Stage 2: test statistical significance via permutation pt <- fragmentation_test(criminology_panel, n_perm = 10000L, seed = 42L) print(pt)  # Stage 3: locate the structural break cp <- fragmentation_changepoint(fr, seed = 42L) print(cp) plot(cp)

title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: apply citefrag data, need two things: set citation matrices indexed time period, vector mapping node group. Optionally also need node sizes (example, article counts) want use size-adjusted normalisations.

title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: canonical input citation_panel() named list square numeric matrices, one per period, identical row column names. entry M[, j] interpreted number citations sent node node j period. Nodes need present every period; node considered active period t appears rownames matrices[[t]]. lets panel expand time new nodes enter, typical citation networks new journals emerge. group vector named character vector whose names match node identifiers. Every node appearing matrix must group assignment. size vector, supplied, can either single named numeric vector (sizes constant time) named list named numeric vectors keyed period (sizes vary time, article counts typically ).

what: Worked example: from CSV to panel
title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: users citation data long form, one row per citing-cited-period triple. helper as_citation_panel() converts directly. Suppose two CSV files: Turning citation_panel takes four lines:
code: citations.csv:     period, from,      to,        value     2018,   journal_A, journal_B, 45     2018,   journal_A, journal_C, 12     2018,   journal_B, journal_A, 23     ...  journals.csv:     journal,   group     journal_A, Theory     journal_B, Methods     journal_C, Applied     ... edges <- read.csv("citations.csv") meta  <- read.csv("journals.csv") groups <- setNames(meta$group, meta$journal) panel <- as_citation_panel(edges, groups = groups)  fragmentation_ratio(panel)

title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: already matrices (example, constructed another data source), pass directly:
code: M2018 <- matrix(c(0, 45, 12,                   23,  0,  8,                    5,  3,  0),                 nrow = 3, byrow = TRUE,                 dimnames = list(c("A","B","C"), c("A","B","C")))  M2019 <- M2018 + 1    # synthetic second year for illustration  panel <- citation_panel(   matrices = list("2018" = M2018, "2019" = M2019),   groups   = c(A = "Theory", B = "Methods", C = "Applied") )

what: Supplying node sizes for size-adjusted normalisations
title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: "target" "article_pair" normalisations, package needs know size node period. annual article counts, pass list named numeric vectors: pass matrices groups, can still use "raw" "row" normalisations, require sizes.
code: sizes <- list(   "2018" = c(A = 180, B = 95, C = 42),   "2019" = c(A = 175, B = 102, C = 48) )  panel <- citation_panel(matrices, groups, sizes = sizes) fragmentation_ratio(panel, normalise = "article_pair")

title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: package exports eight functions. first two construct input object; next four main analytical workflow; last two convenience helpers. citation_panel() constructs citation_panel object list matrices, group vector, optional node sizes. Performs input validation. as_citation_panel() convenience constructor accepts long-form edge list data frame instead pre-built matrices. fragmentation_ratio() computes pair-averaged within-group -group citation ratio period, one four normalisations: raw, row-normalised, target-size adjusted, article-pair normalised. Returns time series optional smoothing. fragmentation_test() runs exact Monte Carlo permutation test null hypothesis within-group preference, holding citation matrix group sizes fixed permuting mapping nodes group labels. fragmentation_robustness() recomputes ratio series alternative group specifications, restricted node subsets, , reports correlations alternative baseline trajectory. fragmentation_changepoint() fits piecewise linear model unknown breakpoint ratio trajectory, using grid search locate breakpoint bootstrap resampling construct confidence interval. asymmetry() measures directional citation flow two specified groups time, reporting share group’s outgoing citations goes group ratio . fragmentation_analysis() runs full pipeline (ratio, test, robustness, changepoint) one call returns list four result objects. Useful reproducing complete analysis. function returns object print(), plot(), summary(), .data.frame() methods. Full argument documentation help pages (example, ?fragmentation_ratio).

what: When to use citefrag, and when not
title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: citefrag narrow design. one thing (measure longitudinal within-group versus -group preference associated inferential machinery) try much else. packages cover adjacent ground, worth naming choose right tool. general bibliometric analysis (co-citation networks, collaboration maps, author productivity, thematic mapping, Lotka’s law), use bibliometrix (Aria Cuccurullo 2017). comprehensive workhorse R-based scientometrics covers enormously ground citefrag . cross-sectional network segregation measures single network (E-index, Freeman’s segregation index, assortativity coefficients), use netseg (Bojanowski). E-index particular classic within-contrast, fine tool data single network rather longitudinal panel need specific statistical scaffolding citefrag provides. low-level network operations (modularity, assortativity, community detection, centrality measures), use igraph (Csárdi Nepusz). specialised network packages, including citefrag internally, build . citefrag right tool question specifically: within-group preference network change time, change statistically distinguishable chance, robust drew group boundaries, structural break? four questions central analysis, package built . question network structure broadly, single-period network, one tools probably better fit.

title: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks
text: citation matrix C(t)C^{(t)} observed period tt, node ii assigned group g()g(), pair-averaged within-ratio R(t)=1|Wt|∑(,j)∈WtCij(t)1|Bt|∑(,j)∈BtCij(t)R(t) = \frac{\frac{1}{|W_t|} \sum_{(,j) \W_t} C^{(t)}_{ij}}{\frac{1}{|B_t|} \sum_{(,j) \B_t} C^{(t)}_{ij}} Wt={(,j):g()=g(j),≠j}W_t = \{(,j) : g() = g(j),\ \neq j\} set directed within-group pairs active period tt, Bt={(,j):g()≠g(j)}B_t = \{(,j) : g() \neq g(j)\} set directed -group pairs. Self-citations excluded. value R(t)=1R(t) = 1 indicates within-group preference; values one indicate nodes cite within group intensely cite across group boundaries. Four normalisations C(t)C^{(t)} supported. raw variant uses counts supplied. row-normalised variant divides row sum, giving every citing node equal weight regardless publication volume. target-size variant additionally divides cell size cited node row-normalising. article-pair variant uses Ωij=Cij/(ni⋅nj)\Omega_{ij} = C_{ij} / (n_i \cdot n_j) directly, removing size effects sides simultaneously. permutation test holds C(t)C^{(t)} group-size vector fixed, permutes mapping nodes labels, computes null distribution R(t)R(t) random assignment. number distinct permutations feasible enumerate, test exact; otherwise approximated Monte Carlo sampling. changepoint procedure fits continuous two-segment linear model grid-searched breakpoint, bounds breakpoint via nonparametric bootstrap observation pairs.

title: Construct a citation panel object — citation_panel
text: matrices named list square citation matrices, one per period. List names period identifiers (typically years character strings). matrix must numeric, square, identical row column names. entry `M[, j]` interpreted number citations node `` node `j` period. groups named character vector mapping node identifiers group labels. names must cover every node appearing matrix `matrices`. Nodes listed `groups` trigger error. sizes Optional node size information size-adjusted normalisations. Either named numeric vector (time-invariant sizes, names matching union nodes) named list named numeric vectors keyed period identifiers (time-varying sizes, annual article counts). Default `NULL`.

what: citefrag: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks — citefrag-package
title: citefrag: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks — citefrag-package
text: citefrag package provides tools measuring within-group versus -group citation preference longitudinal journal panels. implements pair-averaged fragmentation ratio several normalisations, exact Monte Carlo permutation tests, directional asymmetry analysis, suite robustness checks, piecewise regression bootstrap confidence intervals locating structural breaks ratio trajectories.

title: citefrag: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks — citefrag-package
text: Construct [citation_panel()] object list annual citation matrices group vector. Compute ratio series [fragmentation_ratio()]. Test statistical significance [fragmentation_test()]. Check robustness [fragmentation_robustness()]. Detect structural breaks [fragmentation_changepoint()]. function returns object `print()`, `plot()`, `summary()`, `.data.frame()` methods. convenience wrapper [fragmentation_analysis()] runs full pipeline one call returns list four result objects.

title: citefrag: Measuring Fragmentation in Longitudinal Citation Networks — citefrag-package
text: citation matrix \(C^{(t)}\) observed period \(t\), node \(\) assigned group \(g()\), pair-averaged within-ratio $$R(t) = \frac{\frac{1}{|W_t|}\sum_{(,j) \W_t} C^{(t)}_{ij}}                   {\frac{1}{|B_t|}\sum_{(,j) \B_t} C^{(t)}_{ij}}$$ \(W_t\) set directed within-group pairs \(B_t\) set directed -group pairs. Self-citations excluded. value \(R(t) = 1\) indicates within-group preference; values one indicate nodes cite within group intensely across group boundaries.

what: Run the full fragmentation analysis pipeline — fragmentation_analysis
title: Run the full fragmentation analysis pipeline — fragmentation_analysis
text: Convenience wrapper runs [fragmentation_ratio()], [fragmentation_test()], [fragmentation_robustness()], [fragmentation_changepoint()] sequence, returning list four result objects. Useful reproducing complete analysis single call, example replication vignette.

title: Run the full fragmentation analysis pipeline — fragmentation_analysis
code: fragmentation_analysis(   panel,   normalise = c("raw", "row", "target", "article_pair"),   smooth = 3L,   n_perm = 10000L,   alternatives = NULL,   subpanels = NULL,   seed = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Run the full fragmentation analysis pipeline — fragmentation_analysis
text: panel `citation_panel` object. normalise Character, [fragmentation_ratio()]. Default `"raw"`. smooth Integer smoothing window. Default `3L`. n_perm Integer, number Monte Carlo permutations. Default `10000L`. alternatives Optional named list alternative group vectors. Default `NULL`, case robustness check alternative groupings carried . subpanels Optional named list node subsets. Default `NULL`. seed Optional integer seed. Default `NULL`. verbose Logical, print progress messages. Default `TRUE`.

title: Run the full fragmentation analysis pipeline — fragmentation_analysis
text: named list elements `ratio`, `test`, `robustness`,   `changepoint`. `robustness` `NULL` neither `alternatives`   `subpanels` supplied.

title: Run the full fragmentation analysis pipeline — fragmentation_analysis
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(criminology_panel) res <- fragmentation_analysis(criminology_panel, seed = 42L) plot(res$ratio) plot(res$changepoint) } # }

what: Piecewise linear regression with bootstrap confidence interval — fragmentation_changepoint
title: Piecewise linear regression with bootstrap confidence interval — fragmentation_changepoint
text: Fits two-segment linear model unknown breakpoint fragmentation ratio time series. breakpoint located grid search minimising residual sum squares; bootstrap confidence interval obtained resampling observation pairs.

title: Piecewise linear regression with bootstrap confidence interval — fragmentation_changepoint
code: fragmentation_changepoint(   x,   value_column = "ratio_smoothed",   min_edge_gap = 5L,   n_boot = 1000L,   seed = NULL )

title: Piecewise linear regression with bootstrap confidence interval — fragmentation_changepoint
text: x Either `fragmentation_ratio` object, data frame columns `period` `value` (case `value` smoothed unsmoothed ratio). value_column `x` `fragmentation_ratio`, column `x$series` fit. Default `"ratio_smoothed"`. Use `"ratio"` fit unsmoothed series. min_edge_gap Integer, number periods end series excluded candidate breakpoints. Default `5L`. n_boot Integer, number bootstrap replications breakpoint confidence interval. Default `1000L`. seed Optional integer seed. Default `NULL`.

title: Piecewise linear regression with bootstrap confidence interval — fragmentation_changepoint
text: object class `fragmentation_changepoint` elements: breakpoint Estimated breakpoint period. ci Named numeric vector length two: `lo` `hi` (2.5th     97.5th bootstrap percentiles). slope_before Pre-breakpoint slope (change ratio per period). slope_after Post-breakpoint slope. p_before, p_after Two-sided p-values piecewise fit. r_squared Fit \(R^2\). fitted Tibble columns `period`, `observed`, `fitted`. config List arguments used.

title: Piecewise linear regression with bootstrap confidence interval — fragmentation_changepoint
text: piecewise model parameterised $$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 (t - bp) \cdot \mathbb{1}(t \leq bp) +            \beta_2 (t - bp) \cdot \mathbb{1}(t > bp) + \varepsilon_t$$ produces continuous fit separate slopes either side breakpoint. breakpoint chosen integer period interior series minimises residual sum squares.

title: Piecewise linear regression with bootstrap confidence interval — fragmentation_changepoint
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(criminology_panel) fr <- fragmentation_ratio(criminology_panel, normalise = "row") cp <- fragmentation_changepoint(fr, seed = 42L) print(cp) plot(cp) } # }

what: Compute the fragmentation ratio time series — fragmentation_ratio
title: Compute the fragmentation ratio time series — fragmentation_ratio
text: central measurement function package. period panel, computes pair-averaged within-group -group citation ratio requested normalisation, returns ratio series alongside supporting counts optional smoothed trajectory.

title: Compute the fragmentation ratio time series — fragmentation_ratio
code: fragmentation_ratio(   panel,   normalise = c("raw", "row", "target", "article_pair"),   smooth = 3L )

title: Compute the fragmentation ratio time series — fragmentation_ratio
text: panel `citation_panel` object [citation_panel()]. normalise Character, one `"raw"`, `"row"`, `"target"`, `"article_pair"`. Default `"raw"`. See Details. smooth Integer window length centred moving-average smoothing ratio. Default `3L`. Set `1L` disable smoothing.

title: Compute the fragmentation ratio time series — fragmentation_ratio
text: object class `fragmentation_ratio` (inheriting   `citefrag_result`), list elements: series tibble one row per period columns `period`,     `n_nodes`, `within_mean`, `between_mean`, `ratio`,     `ratio_smoothed`. config List recording `normalise`, `smooth`, call. panel input panel, downstream use.

title: Compute the fragmentation ratio time series — fragmentation_ratio
text: **Normalisations.** `"raw"` option uses citation counts supplied. `"row"` divides row matrix row sum aggregation, every citing node contributes equally regardless publication volume. `"target"` additionally divides cell size (example, article count) cited node, controlling differences size citation targets. `"article_pair"` uses \(\Omega_{ij} = C_{ij} / (n_i \cdot n_j)\) directly, removing size effects sides simultaneously. latter two require `sizes` supplied panel constructed. **Smoothing.** `ratio_smoothed` column centred moving average computed observation domain, time-series filter; cosmetic aid plotting rather statistical operation. significance testing uses unsmoothed ratio.

title: Compute the fragmentation ratio time series — fragmentation_ratio
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(criminology_panel) fr <- fragmentation_ratio(criminology_panel, normalise = "row") print(fr) plot(fr) } # }

what: Robustness checks for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_robustness
title: Robustness checks for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_robustness
text: Recomputes ratio series alternative group specifications restricted node subsets, compares alternative baseline. function answers "trajectory depend specific cluster assignment?" "depend nodes include?".

title: Robustness checks for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_robustness
code: fragmentation_robustness(   panel,   alternatives = NULL,   subpanels = NULL,   normalise = c("raw", "row", "target", "article_pair"),   smooth = 3L )

title: Robustness checks for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_robustness
text: panel `citation_panel` object, representing baseline specification. alternatives Optional named list alternative group vectors. element must named character vector node identifiers `panel$groups`, possibly different group labels. example, `list(merge_A_B = c(j1 = "X", j2 = "X", j3 = "Y"))`. subpanels Optional named list node subsets. element character vector node identifiers retain. ratio recomputed subpanel using nodes. normalise Character, [fragmentation_ratio()]. Default `"raw"`. smooth Integer smoothing window. Default `3L`.

title: Robustness checks for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_robustness
text: object class `fragmentation_robustness` elements: series Tibble columns `specification`, `period`, `ratio`,     `ratio_smoothed`, long-form across specifications     (baseline plus alternatives plus subpanels). correlations Tibble one row per non-baseline specification,     columns `specification`, `r_raw`, `r_smooth`: correlation     baseline ratio smoothed baseline ratio. config List arguments used. panel Input panel.

title: Robustness checks for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_robustness
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(criminology_panel) # Alternative specification: merge two groups into one g0 <- criminology_panel$groups alt <- g0; alt[alt == "Intl/Comparative"] <- "Critical/Theoretical" rb <- fragmentation_robustness(   criminology_panel,   alternatives = list(merged_crit_intl = alt) ) print(rb) plot(rb) } # }

what: Permutation test for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_test
title: Permutation test for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_test
text: Tests whether observed within-ratio exceeds obtained random assignment nodes groups sizes. small label sets (number distinct permutations `exact_threshold`) test exact; otherwise approximated Monte Carlo sampling.

title: Permutation test for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_test
code: fragmentation_test(   panel,   n_perm = 10000L,   exact_threshold = 1000L,   normalise = c("raw", "row", "target", "article_pair"),   seed = NULL )

title: Permutation test for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_test
text: panel `citation_panel` object. n_perm Integer, number Monte Carlo permutations exact enumeration infeasible. Default `10000L`. exact_threshold Integer. number distinct permutations group labels value, test carried exact enumeration rather Monte Carlo. Default `1000L`. normalise Character, one `"raw"`, `"row"`, `"target"`, `"article_pair"`. Default `"raw"`. seed Optional integer seed reproducibility Monte Carlo sampling. Default `NULL`.

title: Permutation test for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_test
text: object class `fragmentation_test` (inheriting   `citefrag_result`), list elements: results Tibble one row per period: `period`, `n_nodes`,     `observed`, `null_mean`, `null_sd`, `null_q05`, `null_q95`,     `null_q99`, `z`, `p_value`, `method` ("exact" "monte_carlo"),     `n_distinct_perms`. config List arguments used. panel Input panel.

title: Permutation test for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_test
text: null hypothesis observed pattern within-group preference produced chance, given fixed group sizes observed citation matrix. test holds group sizes citation matrix fixed permutes mapping nodes group labels; permutation, ratio recomputed. z-score \((R_{obs} - \bar{R}_{null}) / \mathrm{sd}(R_{null})\); p-value proportion null ratios least extreme observed. Periods fewer four active nodes, fewer two groups size least two, skipped.

title: Permutation test for the fragmentation ratio — fragmentation_test
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(criminology_panel) pt <- fragmentation_test(criminology_panel, n_perm = 1000L, seed = 42L) print(pt) } # }
